question_text: Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \dots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample of size $n \left( \geq 2 \right)$ from a population having probability density function $ f ( x ; \theta ) = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \theta ( 2 x ) ^ { \theta - 1 } } & { \mathrm { ~ i f ~ } 0 < x \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \\ { \theta ( 2 - 2 x ) ^ { \theta - 1 } } & { \mathrm { ~ i f ~ } \frac { 1 } { 2 } < x \leq 1 } \\ { 0 } & { \mathrm { ~ o t h e r w i s e , } } \end{array} \right. $ where $\theta > 0$ is an unknown parameter. Then which one of the following is a maximum likelihood estimator of ???

options.A: $ n \left[ ~ \sum _ { \left\{ i : X _ { i } \leq { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \right\} } \log _ { \mathrm { e } } ~ 2 X _ { i } + ~ \sum _ { \left\{ i : X _ { i } > { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \right\} } \log _ { \mathrm { e } } ( 2 - 2 X _ { i } ) \right] ^ { - 1 }\tag{B} $ $ \begin{array} { r } { - n \bigg [ \sum _ { \left\{ i : X _ { i } \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right\} } \log _ { \mathrm { e } } { 2 X _ { i } } + \ \sum _ { \left\{ i : X _ { i } > \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right\} } \log _ { \mathrm { e } } ( 2 - 2 X _ { i } ) \bigg ] ^ { - 1 } } \end{array}\tag{C} $ $ n \big [ \sum _ { \{ 1 \leq i \leq n \} } \log _ { \mathrm { e } } { 2 X _ { i } } + \sum _ { \{ 1 \leq i \leq n \} } \log _ { \mathrm { e } } ( 2 - 2 X _ { i } ) \big ] ^ { - 1 }\tag{D} $ $ - n \big [ \sum _ { \{ 1 \leq i \leq n \} } \log _ { \mathrm { e } } { 2 X _ { i } } + \sum _ { \{ 1 \leq i \leq n \} } \log _ { e } ( 2 - 2 X _ { i } ) \big ] ^ { - 1 } $

solution:
To find the maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) of \(\theta\) for the given probability density function (pdf), we need to follow these steps:

1. **Write down the likelihood function:**
   The likelihood function \(L(\theta)\) is the product of the pdf evaluated at each data point \(X_i\):
   \[
   L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^n f(X_i; \theta)
   \]
   Given the pdf:
   \[
   f(x; \theta) = \begin{cases} 
   \theta (2x)^{\theta - 1} & \text{if } 0 < x \leq \frac{1}{2} \\
   \theta (2 - 2x)^{\theta - 1} & \text{if } \frac{1}{2} < x \leq 1 \\
   0 & \text{otherwise}
   \end{cases}
   \]

2. **Express the likelihood function in terms of the data:**
   Let \(k\) be the number of observations such that \(X_i \leq \frac{1}{2}\). Then, \(n - k\) is the number of observations such that \(X_i > \frac{1}{2}\). The likelihood function can be written as:
   \[
   L(\theta) = \left( \prod_{i=1}^k \theta (2X_i)^{\theta - 1} \right) \left( \prod_{i=k+1}^n \theta (2 - 2X_i)^{\theta - 1} \right)
   \]

3. **Simplify the likelihood function:**
   \[
   L(\theta) = \theta^k (2X_1)^{\theta - 1} \cdots (2X_k)^{\theta - 1} \cdot \theta^{n-k} (2 - 2X_{k+1})^{\theta - 1} \cdots (2 - 2X_n)^{\theta - 1}
   \]
   \[
   L(\theta) = \theta^n \left( 2^k \prod_{i=1}^k X_i \right)^{\theta - 1} \left( 2^{n-k} \prod_{i=k+1}^n (1 - X_i) \right)^{\theta - 1}
   \]
   \[
   L(\theta) = \theta^n 2^{k(n-k)} \left( \prod_{i=1}^k X_i \cdot \prod_{i=k+1}^n (1 - X_i) \right)^{\theta - 1}
   \]

4. **Take the natural logarithm of the likelihood function:**
   \[
   \ln L(\theta) = \ln \left( \theta^n 2^{k(n-k)} \left( \prod_{i=1}^k X_i \cdot \prod_{i=k+1}^n (1 - X_i) \right)^{\theta - 1} \right)
   \]
   Using properties of logarithms:
   \[
   \ln L(\theta) = \ln (\theta^n) + \ln (
